text: Introduction

Diagnostic laboratory testing is a foundational component of modern healthcare. Blood counts, lipid profiles, glycated-haemoglobin (HbA1c) measurements, thyroid panels, and tumour markers inform diagnosis, guide treatment, and track the progression of chronic disease over time. In Egypt, however, the experience of obtaining such tests remains largely analogue and fragmented. A patient who needs a specific test typically relies on word of mouth or a physician's referral to choose a laboratory, has no neutral means of comparing the price of an identical test between providers, receives the result as a one-off printed or PDF document, and accumulates no continuous, queryable record of their health markers as they move between laboratories over the years.

**TesTly** is a curated, multi-actor medical lab-testing marketplace for Egypt designed to address this gap. It is a single platform on which a patient can discover accredited diagnostic laboratories nearby, compare the same test across many laboratories by price, rating, distance, and accreditation, book that test through one of three collection channels, pay for it online or in cash, receive the official result both as a secure PDF and as structured, machine-readable data, and track health markers over time across results issued by different laboratories. The system models three human roles through a single `Role` enumeration — **Patient**, **Lab Staff**, and **Admin** — and supplements them with a non-human, patient-facing AI assistant.

TesTly is delivered as two client applications over one shared application programming interface (API). A **Next.js** web application serves the public storefront together with the patient, lab-staff, and admin workspaces, while a native **Flutter** mobile application serves patients and lab staff on iOS and Android. Both clients consume a single **NestJS** backend backed by a **PostgreSQL** database accessed through the **Prisma** object-relational mapper. The backend additionally integrates four external services: object storage for result documents, Firebase Cloud Messaging for push notifications, and Google's Gemini generative-AI model that powers an agentic, function-calling health assistant grounded in the live catalogue. The overall structure of the platform, its clients, backend modules, and external dependencies is shown in the architecture diagram.

caption: High-level architecture of TesTly: two clients (web and mobile) over a single NestJS API, which fronts a PostgreSQL database and four external services.

text: The platform is distinctive in three respects. First, it is a *curated* marketplace: a laboratory cannot transact until an administrator has verified that it is operationally complete, so every bookable laboratory exposed to patients meets a concrete readiness standard. Second, it solves the cross-laboratory continuity problem through a clinical-normalisation engine that converts each laboratory's differently named and differently united analytes into a canonical, comparable form, governed by a patient-controlled privacy setting. Third, it layers a guard-railed conversational assistant over the real catalogue so that help is both natural-language and factually grounded. Because the domain is medical, security and privacy are treated as first-class concerns throughout the design.
Problem Statement and Motivation
The market for diagnostic laboratory testing in Egypt is fragmented and opaque, which produces several concrete difficulties for patients, laboratories, and the ecosystem as a whole.

**Fragmented lab discovery.** There is no neutral, comprehensive directory through which a patient can find the laboratories that offer a particular test near them. Discovery depends on informal referral, advertising, or prior familiarity, none of which lets a patient survey the full set of available options or read other patients' experiences before deciding.

**No price transparency.** The price of an identical test — a complete blood count, a lipid profile, an HbA1c — varies widely between laboratories and is rarely published in a form that allows side-by-side comparison. A patient therefore cannot easily determine whether a quoted price is reasonable, and competition on price is muted by the absence of a comparison venue.

**Paper and one-off digital results.** Results are typically delivered as isolated printed sheets or standalone PDF files. Such documents are not machine-readable: their values cannot be queried, charted, or compared, and the patient is left to interpret each report in isolation.

**No cross-lab history.** Because results are isolated artefacts and because each laboratory names and units its analytes differently, a patient's longitudinal history does not follow them when they switch laboratories. There is no privacy-respecting mechanism by which a marker measured at one laboratory can be trended against the same marker measured at another, even though such longitudinal tracking is precisely what is clinically useful for chronic-disease management.

**Manual booking and settlement.** Appointment scheduling, capability checks (for example, whether a laboratory offers home sample collection), payment, and status updates are handled manually or by telephone, which is slow, error-prone, and offers the patient no visibility into the state of their order.

For laboratories, especially smaller ones, the corresponding gap is the absence of a low-cost storefront, scheduling, and results-delivery system. For the ecosystem as a whole, there is no trusted intermediary that verifies that a laboratory is operationally complete before exposing it to patients. The motivation behind TesTly is to digitise this entire journey end to end: to give patients a transparent, comparison-first marketplace; to give laboratories a complete back-office behind an administrator-gated onboarding process; and to convert isolated paper results into structured, normalised, trendable data under the patient's control.
Project Objectives

The principal objective of this project is to design and implement a curated, multi-actor medical lab-testing marketplace that takes a patient from discovery through booking, payment, and result delivery to longitudinal health tracking, while giving laboratories a complete operational workspace and giving administrators the tools to govern the platform. This overarching goal decomposes into the following specific objectives.

items: **Provide a unified, transparent marketplace.** Offer a single public storefront, browsable without registration, on which patients can search and filter accredited laboratories by free-text query, city, rating, distance, price, accreditation, and home-collection capability, and compare a single named test across every laboratory that offers it, ranked by price, with each laboratory's own price, rating, accreditation, and location.

**Support multi-channel booking and payment.** Allow a patient to book a test through one of three collection modes — lab visit, home sample collection, or home test kit — selecting a future schedule slot where applicable, and to settle payment either online or through one of three cash modes (cash at the lab, cash to the home collector, or cash on delivery), with the booking lifecycle, payment state, and kit-shipment progress tracked and visible end to end.

**Deliver digital and structured results with cross-lab trends.** Deliver each result both as an authoritative PDF, served only through an authorisation-checked endpoint, and as structured observations that are normalised to canonical analytes and units, so that a patient can view longitudinal trends for a marker across results issued by different laboratories, with reference-range shading and abnormal-value flagging, governed by a patient-controlled privacy setting.

**Provide AI-guided discovery.** Layer an agentic, function-calling AI assistant over the live catalogue so that a patient can ask, in natural language and in either English or Arabic, where to obtain a test and what it means, and receive grounded answers and clickable laboratory and test cards that lead directly into the booking funnel, complemented by a lightweight deterministic FAQ helper for common questions.

**Implement a secure, role-based platform.** Serve three roles — patient, lab staff, and admin — through layered, defence-in-depth access control, ensure that only laboratories which pass a concrete operational-readiness gate become bookable, and treat the confidentiality of medical data as a first-class design concern across both the web and mobile clients.

text: In meeting these objectives the system is intended to demonstrate a coherent, end-to-end pipeline in which a patient can move from an AI-assisted conversation to a compared, booked, paid, delivered, and trended test, and in which a laboratory can move from a gated registration to a fully operational storefront, all on one curated platform.
Thesis Organization
The remainder of this thesis is organised as follows.

**Chapter 2 — Related Work.** This chapter situates TesTly within the landscape of medical diagnostic services and digital-health technology. It surveys the domain background and the global rise of e-health marketplaces, reviews the categories of existing applications and the representative real-world systems in each — together with the gaps they leave — and consolidates that survey into a feature-comparison matrix. It then justifies the engineering choices made for TesTly by comparing every layer of the technology stack against its principal alternatives, and closes with the platform's business model and market positioning.

**Chapter 3 — Methodologies and Tools.** This chapter presents the engineering methodologies that shaped the platform — layered, modular backend architecture, contract-first REST design, database-first modelling, mobile clean architecture, and security- and privacy-by-design — then catalogues the concrete development tools used in each subsystem. It closes with the project-management approach: the agile, phased delivery rhythm, the Git-based version-control workflow, and the project's risk-management register.

**Chapter 4 — Design and Implementation.** This chapter establishes the system's requirements and then realises them. It captures the functional requirements as per-actor user stories with accompanying use-case diagrams, sets out the non-functional requirements, and presents the end-to-end user workflows. It then details the user-interface design of both clients; the layered runtime and deployment architecture; the relational data model of nineteen tables and eleven enumerations, with its distinctive structured-results design; the REST API surface; and the authentication and authorisation model — before walking through the implementation of the core and advanced features in code.

**Chapter 5 — Testing and Evaluation.** This chapter describes how the platform was validated. It presents the layer-appropriate testing strategy (Jest on the backend, Playwright on the web, and flutter_test on mobile) and a summary of the project's test metrics, evaluates the delivered user experience, works through a concrete end-to-end case study that shows the system solving a real patient problem, and honestly documents the advantages the system offers over the status quo together with the limitations that remain.

**Chapter 6 — Conclusions and Future Work.** This chapter summarises the contributions of the project, evaluates the extent to which the stated objectives were met, reflects on the limitations of the current implementation — such as the simulated payment gateway — and outlines directions for future work, including a production payment integration and broader clinical interoperability.
